guide/festivals.json.title: Festivals & events
guide/festivals.json.subtitle: 39 celebrations through the year — pick a day to see what's on.

festivals.name: Founding Day of Saudi Arabia
festivals.description: National holiday commemorating the founding of the first Saudi state in 1727. Celebrated with parades, cultural events, and flag displays across the kingdom.

festivals.name: Janadriyah Festival
festivals.description: One of the world's largest cultural festivals showcasing Saudi heritage, traditional crafts, poetry, and folk performances. A major celebration of Arabian culture and identity.

festivals.name: Eid al-Fitr
festivals.description: Islamic festival marking the end of Ramadan with three days of celebration, family gatherings, and festivities. Public holiday with special prayers and traditional meals.

festivals.name: Asir Flowers Festival
festivals.description: Celebration of Asir's blooming season in the mountains with colorful flower displays and cultural performances. A picturesque festival showcasing the region's natural beauty.

festivals.name: Thallaj Winter Festival
festivals.description: Mountain festival in Asir celebrating the cooler climate and natural landscapes with activities, traditional crafts, and cultural shows. A unique summer escape destination.

festivals.name: Eid al-Adha
festivals.description: Major Islamic festival lasting four days commemorating Prophet Ibrahim's sacrifice. Marked by prayer services, animal sacrifices, feasts, and family celebrations nationwide.

festivals.name: Hajj Season
festivals.description: The world's largest annual religious gathering where millions of Muslims perform pilgrimage to the holiest sites in Islam. A profound spiritual and cultural phenomenon.

festivals.name: Saudi National Day
festivals.description: Annual celebration of Saudi Arabia's unification in 1932 with public holiday, fireworks, parades, and cultural performances. The kingdom's most important national celebration.

festivals.name: Riyadh Season
festivals.description: Three-month entertainment festival featuring concerts, cultural performances, food festivals, and family activities. A massive celebration of arts and culture in the capital.

festivals.name: Jeddah Season
festivals.description: Coastal entertainment festival featuring music, theater, food, and art events along the Red Sea. A cultural celebration unique to the port city.

festivals.name: Qassim Dates Festival
festivals.description: Annual celebration of Qassim's renowned date harvest with tastings, competitions, and traditional market displays. A showcase of the region's agricultural heritage.

festivals.name: Jizan Summer Festival
festivals.description: Coastal festival celebrating the season with marine activities, cultural shows, and traditional performances. Features the unique maritime heritage of southern Saudi Arabia.

festivals.name: Tantora Governorate Festival
festivals.description: Winter cultural festival in Al Khobar featuring music, theater, heritage events, and culinary experiences. A major celebration of contemporary and traditional arts.

festivals.name: Ha'il Equestrian Festival
festivals.description: Premier horse racing and equestrian sports festival celebrating Arabian horse culture and desert traditions. A highlight for equestrian enthusiasts worldwide.

festivals.name: Almajmaah Heritage Festival
festivals.description: Festival celebrating the historic Almajmaah region with traditional crafts, cultural performances, and heritage demonstrations. Showcases Najdi culture and history.

festivals.name: Tabuk Date & Olive Festival
festivals.description: Northern region's harvest festival featuring date and olive products with tastings, competitions, and agricultural displays. Celebrates the region's farming traditions.

festivals.name: Northern Borders Heritage Festival
festivals.description: Cultural celebration in Arar showcasing Bedouin traditions, crafts, poetry, and folklore of northern Saudi Arabia. Highlights the unique desert heritage of the region.

festivals.name: Najran Heritage Festival
festivals.description: Annual celebration of Najran's historical significance with traditional performances, handicrafts, and cultural displays. Showcases the southern region's unique identity.

festivals.name: Taif Rose Festival
festivals.description: Celebration of Taif's famous Damascus roses with floral displays, rose water products, and cultural events. A fragrant showcase of the region's agricultural specialty.

festivals.name: Medina Dates Festival
festivals.description: Celebration of Madinah's premium Ajwa dates during harvest season with exhibitions and tastings. Honors the city's most famous agricultural product.

festivals.name: Eastern Province Food Festival
festivals.description: Celebration of Gulf coastal cuisine with traditional dishes, cooking demonstrations, and cultural entertainment. Showcases the region's unique culinary heritage.

festivals.name: Islamic New Year
festivals.description: Religious observance marking the beginning of the Islamic calendar with special prayers and religious gatherings. A significant date in the Islamic year.

festivals.name: Mawlid al-Nabi (Prophet's Birthday)
festivals.description: Religious celebration of Prophet Muhammad's birth with special prayers, gatherings, and religious lectures. An important date in Islamic tradition.

festivals.name: Ramadan Month
festivals.description: The holiest month in Islam dedicated to fasting, prayer, and spiritual reflection from dawn to sunset. Marked by special evening prayers, family gatherings, and cultural activities.

festivals.name: Asir Coffee Festival
festivals.description: Celebration of traditional Arabian coffee culture with roasting demonstrations, tastings, and cultural performances. Honors the region's coffee heritage.

festivals.name: Jeddah International Film Festival
festivals.description: Major international film festival showcasing regional and international cinema with screenings and cultural events. A premier event for film enthusiasts.

festivals.name: Riyadh Contemporary Art Biennale
festivals.description: International contemporary art exhibition featuring installations, performances, and avant-garde works. Celebrates modern artistic expression in the capital.

festivals.name: Madinah Heritage Festival
festivals.description: Festival celebrating Madinah's rich Islamic history and heritage with cultural exhibits, educational programs, and traditional performances. Honors the city's spiritual significance.

festivals.name: Qassim Poetry Festival
festivals.description: Literary celebration featuring traditional and contemporary Arabic poetry with competitions, readings, and cultural discussions. A premier event for poetry enthusiasts.

festivals.name: Makkah Umrah Season
festivals.description: Peak season for Umrah pilgrimage attracting millions of visitors to the holy city for this lesser pilgrimage. A year-round spiritual journey with special significance during holidays.

festivals.name: Jizan Mango Festival
festivals.description: Celebration of Jizan's mango harvest with tastings, competitions, and agricultural exhibitions. Showcases the region's tropical fruit heritage.

festivals.name: Ha'il Handicrafts Festival
festivals.description: Festival celebrating traditional craftsmanship with textile weaving, pottery, and woodwork displays. Preserves and promotes the region's artisanal heritage.

festivals.name: Eastern Province Pearl Heritage Festival
festivals.description: Celebration of the region's pearl diving heritage with cultural performances, exhibitions, and maritime traditions. Honors the historical pearl industry.

festivals.name: Tabuk Winter Festival
festivals.description: Northern festival celebrating winter season with outdoor activities, cultural shows, and traditional market experiences. Features the unique culture of northwestern Saudi Arabia.

festivals.name: Najran Folk Arts Festival
festivals.description: Celebration of traditional folk arts and performances unique to southern Saudi culture. Features traditional music, dance, and theatrical presentations.

festivals.name: Alula Arts Festival
festivals.description: Contemporary art festival in the historic AlUla region combining modern artistic expression with ancient heritage sites. A unique blend of art and archaeology.

festivals.name: Riyadh Golf Cup
festivals.description: Premier golf tournament attracting international players to compete in championship events. A major sporting celebration in the capital.

festivals.name: Asir Mountain Festival
festivals.description: Adventure and cultural festival in the mountains featuring outdoor activities, traditional shows, and scenic celebrations. Highlights the region's natural beauty and culture.

festivals.name: Madinah Islamic Culture Week
festivals.description: Week-long celebration of Islamic heritage with lectures, exhibitions, and cultural programs. Emphasizes Madinah's role in Islamic civilization.

guide/history.json.title: A Short History of Saudi Arabia
guide/history.json.subtitle: From its beginnings to today — 13 eras that shaped it.

items.label: Neolithic Settlement
items.summary: Early humans establish permanent settlements across the Arabian Peninsula as climate permits agriculture and herding.
items.text: Archaeological evidence shows that hunter-gatherers transitioned to settled communities in the Fertile Crescent and along Arabian coastlines during the Neolithic period. These pioneering settlements practiced rudimentary agriculture and domesticated animals, taking advantage of seasonal water sources and arable land. The development of irrigation techniques allowed populations to thrive in otherwise arid regions. These early societies laid the foundation for trade networks that would eventually connect the Arabian Peninsula to Egypt, Mesopotamia, and the Indus Valley.

items.label: Bronze Age Kingdoms
items.summary: Bronze-working civilizations emerge, including the Dilmun kingdom, establishing trade routes across the Gulf and Indian Ocean.
items.text: The Bronze Age saw the rise of sophisticated trading kingdoms throughout Arabia, particularly Dilmun in the eastern Gulf, which became a crucial middleman in commerce between Mesopotamia and the Indus Valley. Archaeological sites reveal advanced urban planning, extensive maritime trade networks, and the production of high-quality bronze tools and weapons. The kingdom of Magan in Oman also flourished during this period, exporting copper and incense across the known world. These early Arabian civilizations developed written records, established laws, and created the infrastructure for a thriving merchant culture that would characterize the region for millennia.

items.label: Iron Age and Incense Trade
items.summary: Iron technology spreads, and the Arabian incense trade becomes a major economic force linking Arabia to Mediterranean civilizations.
items.text: The introduction of iron working technology transformed Arabian societies, enabling better agricultural tools and weapons while reducing dependence on distant copper sources. The discovery and cultivation of frankincense and myrrh in southern Arabia made these regions phenomenally wealthy, as Mediterranean and Eastern civilizations prized these aromatics for religious ceremonies and medicine. Caravan routes expanded dramatically, with merchants establishing trade stations across the peninsula and creating the infrastructure for the famous Incense Route. These developments attracted international attention and investment, establishing Arabia as a vital hub in global commerce and making control of trade routes a matter of strategic importance for empires across the Mediterranean and Middle East.

items.label: Pre-Islamic Arabian Kingdoms
items.summary: Competing Arab kingdoms including the Nabataeans, Himyarites, and Lakhmids dominate the peninsula with distinct political and religious systems.
items.text: The centuries before Islam saw Arabia divided among several powerful kingdoms, each controlling valuable trade routes and territories. The Nabataeans in the northwest built the magnificent city of Petra and dominated caravan trade, while the Himyarite kingdom in Yemen controlled the incense routes and adopted Judaism as a state religion. In the north, the Lakhmid kingdom served as a buffer state between the Arabian Peninsula and the Persian and Byzantine empires. These kingdoms developed sophisticated urban centers, maintained diplomatic relations with distant powers, and created their own scripts and cultural traditions, demonstrating that pre-Islamic Arabia was far from a cultural wasteland but rather a complex tapestry of competing civilizations.

items.label: Rise of Islam
items.summary: Muhammad is born in Mecca and founds Islam, fundamentally transforming Arabian society and launching a religious and political movement that reshapes the world.
items.text: Muhammad ibn Abdullah was born in Mecca around 570 AD into a merchant family during a period of religious and social upheaval in Arabia. Around 610 AD, he began receiving revelations that became the Quran, and he gradually attracted followers to his monotheistic message of Islam, which challenged the existing polytheistic practices and power structures of Mecca. His teachings emphasized social justice, charity, and submission to God, attracting both the poor and some merchants seeking reform. Initially persecuted by the Meccan elite, Muhammad and his followers migrated to Medina in 622 AD, an event known as the Hijra that marks the beginning of the Islamic calendar and the establishment of the first Islamic state.

items.label: Early Caliphate Expansion
items.summary: Under the Rightly Guided and Umayyad caliphs, Islam spreads rapidly across the Middle East, North Africa, and into Central Asia through military conquest and conversion.
items.text: Following Muhammad's death in 632 AD, the caliphate expanded with remarkable speed, transforming Arabia from a collection of regional kingdoms into the heartland of a vast empire. The Rightly Guided Caliphs (632–661) and the subsequent Umayyad Dynasty (661–750) conquered Syria, Persia, Egypt, and North Africa, creating an empire that stretched from Spain to India within just over a century. This rapid expansion was driven by religious fervor, economic incentive, and superior military organization, though it also incorporated existing administrative systems and allowed various populations to maintain their own religious practices. The establishment of Islam as the state religion unified Arabia in ways that no previous ruler had achieved, ending the fragmentation of the peninsula and elevating it to the center of a new world civilization.

items.label: Abbasid Period and Fragmentation
items.summary: The Abbasid Caliphate rules from Baghdad while regional dynasties fragment the Arabian Peninsula, including the rise of the Fatimids and various tribal confederations.
items.text: The Abbasid Revolution of 750 AD shifted the Islamic empire's center of power from Damascus to Baghdad, initiating a period of cultural flowering but also political fragmentation that directly affected Arabia. While the Abbasids nominally controlled the Arabian Peninsula, actual power devolved to regional dynasties and local emirs, particularly in Yemen where the Ismaili Fatimids and later the Zaydi dynasty established powerful independent states. Central and northern Arabia fell under the control of various Bedouin confederations and smaller emirates, each maintaining semi-autonomous rule and collecting taxes from pilgrims traveling to Mecca and Medina. The fragmentation, while reducing centralized power, allowed for significant cultural and economic development, as pilgrimage to the holy cities became increasingly important and wealthy merchant families established dynastic control over key trading centers.

items.label: Mamluk Guardianship
items.summary: The Mamluk Sultanate controls Mecca and Medina while various dynasties rule the rest of Arabia, maintaining the holy cities' importance through pilgrimage revenues and Islamic authority.
items.text: When the Mongols destroyed the Abbasid Caliphate in 1258, the Mamluk Sultanate of Egypt emerged as the protector of Islam's holiest sites and the dominant power in the eastern Mediterranean and Arabian Peninsula. The Mamluks appointed governors to Mecca and Medina and collected substantial revenues from the annual pilgrimage while maintaining the prestige associated with guarding Islamic sacred spaces. Meanwhile, southern and central Arabia were ruled by various dynasties including the Rasulids in Yemen and the Juhayna tribes in the Hijaz, each maintaining a degree of independence while acknowledging Mamluk overlordship. The protection of pilgrimage routes and the administration of the holy cities remained a central concern, as Mecca's spiritual importance ensured that whoever controlled it wielded significant religious authority throughout the Islamic world.

items.label: Ottoman Control
items.summary: The Ottoman Empire annexes Arabia, with Mecca and Medina under direct imperial control, while the vast interior remains populated by autonomous Bedouin tribes.
items.text: The Ottoman conquest of Egypt and the Levant in 1517 extended Istanbul's control over the Arabian Peninsula's western coast and, crucially, the holy cities of Mecca and Medina, which became Ottoman imperial possessions of tremendous religious significance. The Ottomans invested heavily in maintaining the hajj infrastructure, constructing fortresses, wells, and caravanserais to protect pilgrims traveling along established routes, while Turkish governors administered the holy cities with bureaucratic efficiency. However, the vast interior deserts of the Arabian Peninsula remained beyond Ottoman administrative reach, with Bedouin tribes maintaining their traditional pastoral lifestyle and tribal governance systems. This division created a two-tier Arabia: a coastal and urban Ottoman periphery focused on commerce and pilgrimage, and a vast interior dominated by autonomous tribal confederations with their own laws, trade practices, and religious interpretations.

items.label: Wahhabi Movement
items.summary: Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab launches an Islamic reform movement that allies with tribal leader Muhammad ibn Saud, creating a powerful new theological and political force.
items.text: In 1744, a theologian named Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab began preaching a puritanical interpretation of Islam that rejected what he saw as corruptions and superstitions that had accumulated over centuries, including saint veneration and elaborate rituals. His teachings found a powerful ally in Muhammad ibn Saud, a local tribal leader, and together they launched a movement that combined religious reform with military conquest, spreading Wahhabi Islam across much of central Arabia. The movement gained popularity among Bedouin tribes seeking a clear, text-based religious framework and appealed to those resenting Ottoman religious laxity and taxation. By the early 19th century, Wahhabi forces had conquered much of Arabia and were threatening Ottoman-controlled territories, forcing Istanbul to send armies to reassert control, though the theological movement proved far more durable than any military campaign could suppress.

items.label: Ibn Saud's Conquest
items.summary: Abdulaziz ibn Saud leads a military campaign to reunify the Arabian Peninsula under Wahhabi Islamic rule, establishing the modern Saudi state.
items.text: In 1902, the young Abdulaziz ibn Saud captured Riyadh with just a handful of followers, beginning a three-decade military campaign that would eventually unite the fragmented Arabian Peninsula into a single kingdom. Drawing on Wahhabi religious ideology and tribal military organization, ibn Saud systematically conquered central Arabia, the Hijaz (containing Mecca and Medina), and the Eastern Province, defeating rival tribal confederations and Ottoman-allied emirs through a combination of warfare and strategic alliances. The conquest was facilitated by the collapse of Ottoman authority during World War I and ibn Saud's shrewd diplomacy with the British, who sought a strong ally in the region to counter other powers. By 1932, after incorporating the last remaining independent territories, Abdulaziz proclaimed himself King of Saudi Arabia, creating the first unified Arabian state in centuries and establishing a kingdom explicitly based on Wahhabi Islamic principles combined with tribal governance traditions.

items.label: Oil Discovery and Modernization
items.summary: The discovery of vast petroleum reserves transforms Saudi Arabia from an isolated desert kingdom into a global economic power and major player in international politics.
items.text: In 1938, the Standard Oil Company of California discovered enormous oil reserves in Saudi Arabia's Eastern Province, fundamentally altering the kingdom's economic and strategic position. The Saudi government, initially cautious about foreign involvement, gradually expanded oil production and negotiated increasingly favorable terms with international oil companies, eventually gaining majority ownership of production through ARAMCO. Oil revenues allowed King Ibn Saud and his successor, King Saud, to modernize infrastructure, fund education, and expand the military, though the rapid changes created tension between traditionalists seeking to preserve Islamic values and modernizers pushing for Western-style development. The 1973 OPEC oil embargo, imposed during the Arab-Israeli War, demonstrated Saudi Arabia's newfound geopolitical leverage and generated wealth unprecedented in Middle Eastern history, setting the stage for the kingdom to become a dominant regional power.

items.label: Modern Kingdom Era
items.summary: Saudi Arabia emerges as a global energy superpower, regional military leader, and Islamic authority, navigating rapid modernization while maintaining conservative traditions.
items.text: Since 1973, Saudi Arabia has leveraged its oil wealth to become the world's largest petroleum exporter and a crucial player in global politics, though this role has brought both unprecedented prosperity and complex challenges. Under King Faisal and subsequent monarchs, the kingdom invested heavily in infrastructure, education, and military modernization while maintaining Wahhabi Islam as the state religion and investing substantially in spreading Islamic influence worldwide through educational institutions and charitable organizations. The kingdom has weathered numerous crises including the Iranian Revolution, the Soviet invasion of Afghanistan, the Gulf War, and ongoing regional conflicts, while grappling with internal tensions between traditional Islamic values and modern aspirations, exemplified in recent years by women's rights reforms, entertainment developments, and Vision 2030 economic diversification initiatives. Today, Saudi Arabia remains a crucial Middle Eastern power, home to Islam's holiest sites, a major military force, and a country struggling to balance its oil-dependent economy with long-term sustainability while facing questions about governance, human rights, and

guide/food.json.title: Food & Drink
guide/food.json.subtitle: How to eat well in Saudi Arabia — customs, dishes and where to go.

items.label: Arabic coffee is not espresso
items.text: Saudi qahwa is a delicate, cardamom-infused brew served in tiny cups without sugar. It's a social ritual, not a caffeine hit—expect to sip slowly while chatting, and always accept at least one cup when offered as a guest.

items.label: The cup-shaking signal
items.text: When you've had enough coffee, gently shake the small cup side-to-side while handing it back to your host. If you keep holding it still, they'll refill it—potentially multiple times.

items.label: Traditional coffee houses (Maqha)
items.text: Visit older neighborhoods in Riyadh or Jeddah for authentic maqhas where locals drink qahwa, smoke shisha, and play cards. These are male-dominated spaces; women may feel uncomfortable in the most traditional venues.

items.label: Dates with coffee
items.text: Coffee is almost always served with dates or small pastries. This pairing is both practical and traditional—the sweetness complements the cardamom flavor.

items.label: Kabsa
items.text: Fragrant rice layered with meat (chicken, lamb, or goat), dried limes, and spices, served with a tangy tomato sauce. It's the national dish and available everywhere from street stalls to fine dining—look for versions with locally-sourced lamb for the best flavor.

items.label: Shawarma
items.text: Spit-roasted meat wrapped in thin bread with tahini, tomatoes, and pickles. Grab it from street vendors in Riyadh's Olaya district or Jeddah's Balad for authentic versions; tourist restaurants often oversalt it.

items.label: Mutabbaq
items.text: A crispy, flaky pastry stuffed with spiced meat, cheese, or spinach, often drizzled with honey. It's a breakfast staple in local cafes and surprisingly filling—one piece can be a full meal.

items.label: Kibbeh
items.text: Ground meat mixed with bulgur and spices, formed into balls or patties and either fried or baked. The fried version is crispier and more common in street food; baked versions are lighter.

items.label: Falafel and hummus
items.text: Crispy chickpea fritters served with creamy hummus, tahini, and pickled vegetables. While not uniquely Saudi, local versions are excellent and far cheaper than Western equivalents.

items.label: Luqaimat
items.text: Sweet, fluffy dough balls fried and drizzled with date syrup or honey, especially popular during Ramadan. Find these at bakeries or dessert shops, and eat them fresh while still warm.

items.label: Street food vendors and souks
items.text: The cheapest and most authentic meals are found in Riyadh's old Souk Al Zal or Jeddah's Balad district. Arrive early (before 11am) for the best selection and freshest food; avoid peak lunch hours if you dislike crowds.

items.label: Family-run local restaurants
items.text: These are marked by Arabic signage only and serve enormous portions of home-style food at 20-30 SAR per meal. Ask your hotel concierge for recommendations in your area, as they rarely appear online.

items.label: Tourist-oriented restaurants in Riyadh and Jeddah
items.text: Chain restaurants and upscale venues in Olaya (Riyadh) and the Corniche (Jeddah) serve international and 'elevated' Saudi cuisine but charge 60-150 SAR per dish. The novelty isn't always worth the markup.

items.label: Restaurants close during prayer times
items.text: Many local eateries shut for 30-45 minutes at each of the five daily prayers. Plan meals accordingly, or stick to restaurants in Western hotels that stay open throughout the day.

items.label: Mall food courts
items.text: Modern shopping malls (The Avenues in Riyadh, Red Sea Mall in Jeddah) house international chains alongside local spots—reliable but less atmospheric. They're good for travelers who find street food overwhelming.

items.label: English menus are inconsistent
items.text: High-end restaurants and chains have English menus, but local spots may only offer Arabic or transliterated names. Use Google Translate's camera function, or ask staff to point to popular dishes on display or other tables.

items.label: Meat is the centerpiece
items.text: Saudi cuisine is heavily meat-based; vegetarian options are limited outside upscale restaurants. If you're vegetarian, learn how to ask for dishes without meat, or stick to hummus, falafel, and salads.

items.label: Family-style portions and sharing
items.text: Restaurants typically serve large platters designed for 2-4 people. Order less than you think you need, and don't hesitate to ask for a half portion or smaller serving.

items.label: No alcohol on menus
items.text: Saudi Arabia prohibits alcohol in all restaurants and public spaces. Any 'wine list' will be non-alcoholic beverages; stick to fresh juices, Arabic coffee, or tea.

items.label: Lunch specials (Tiffin) are excellent value
items.text: Between 12-2pm, many restaurants offer fixed-price lunch combos (25-40 SAR) with soup, main, rice, and bread—far better value than à la carte ordering.

items.label: Fresh juices are exceptional
items.text: Juice bars (أسير asir) are ubiquitous and serve freshly pressed orange, mango, pomegranate, and carrot juices for 5-10 SAR. Order without added sugar if you prefer less sweetness—the fruit itself is naturally sweet.

items.label: Tamarind drink (Tamarind Hindi)
items.text: A tangy, sweet beverage made from tamarind pulp, popular during Ramadan and summer months. It's refreshing and slightly medicinal-tasting; order it cold with crushed ice.

items.label: Hibiscus tea (Karkade)
items.text: A deep red, tart herbal tea served hot or cold, believed to have health benefits. It's common in traditional coffee houses and is caffeine-free.

items.label: Bottled water is essential
items.text: Tap water is safe to drink in cities, but most locals and visitors drink bottled water. Carry a bottle with you, as dehydration is a real concern in the desert heat.

items.label: Alcohol is completely prohibited
items.text: There are no bars, wine shops, or alcoholic beverages anywhere in Saudi Arabia—not even in Western hotels or duty-free shops. This is a strict, non-negotiable law enforced with heavy penalties.

items.label: Eat with your right hand only
items.text: The left hand is traditionally considered unclean in Saudi culture. Even if you're left-handed, make a genuine effort to eat with your right hand, or at least use it for communal dishes.

items.label: Remove shoes before sitting on the floor
items.text: Many traditional restaurants feature majlis-style seating with cushions on the floor. Shoes come off before you sit, and your feet should point away from other diners—never toward people or food.

items.label: Refuse food politely three times
items.text: If you're offered food as a guest and genuinely don't want it, say 'no thank you' twice more after your first refusal. On the third refusal, hosts usually accept; accepting too readily suggests you didn't really want it.

items.label: Do not touch or comment on women negatively
items.text: If dining with mixed company, maintain respectful distance and avoid physical contact. Keep conversation appropriate and never discuss alcohol, pork, or dating culture.

items.label: Compliment the food and host
items.text: Saying 'Alhamdulillah' (thanks be to God) or 'Tisbah ala khair' (good night/be well) after eating shows respect. A simple 'Shukran' (thank you) goes a long way.

items.label: Ramadan dining changes everything
items.text: During the holy month, eating, drinking, and smoking in public is forbidden during daylight hours—even for non-Muslims. Restaurants close or serve only after sunset; plan meals accordingly.

items.label: Tipping is not obligatory but appreciated
items.text: Service charges are sometimes included in restaurant bills (check your receipt). A 10-15% tip for good service is appreciated but not expected, and cash tips are preferred to card additions.

items.label: Cash payments are still common
items.text: While cards are accepted at upscale restaurants and malls, street vendors and local eateries often demand cash (SAR). Withdraw money from ATMs in advance to avoid issues.

items.label: Pork and shellfish are prohibited
items.text: Due to Islamic dietary laws (Halal), pork is never served, and shellfish is rare. Chicken, lamb, goat, and beef are the standards; seafood is available in coastal cities like Jeddah but usually fish-based.

items.label: Ask about meal ingredients if you have allergies
items.text: Many dishes contain nuts, sesame, or hidden ingredients. If you have serious allergies, learn key phrases in Arabic or use a translation app to communicate with kitchen staff.

guide/transport.json.title: Getting Around
guide/transport.json.subtitle: Trains, roads, taxis and how to move around Saudi Arabia.

items.label: Saudi Railways
items.text: The Saudi Railway Company operates the main network connecting Riyadh, Dammam, and Khobar. Trains are modern, comfortable, and significantly cheaper than flying, though journey times are lengthy (8+ hours Riyadh to Dammam).

items.label: Booking tickets
items.text: Purchase tickets online via the Saudi Railways website or mobile app, or at station counters. Book in advance during peak travel times, especially weekends and holidays.
ID verification is required at check-in; carry your passport or Iqama (residence permit). Luggage allowances are generous but oversized bags may incur fees.
Train strikes occasionally occur without much notice; check recent travel updates before planning a long journey. The network is limited compared to other GCC countries.

items.label: Riyadh Metro
items.text: The modern Riyadh Metro (opened 2020) covers six lines with stations across the city. It's fast, air-conditioned, and far cheaper than taxis; single journeys cost 1–3 SAR depending on distance.

items.label: Hayat card
items.text: Get a reloadable Hayat card from metro stations for convenience; tap to enter and exit. Tourist cards are also available at kiosks in main stations.

items.label: Bus networks
items.text: SAPTCO operates intercity coaches; local city buses run in Riyadh (SAPTCO), Jeddah, and Dammam. Buses are affordable and air-conditioned, though schedules can be irregular.
Women-only sections exist on metro cars and buses; respect these designations. During prayer times, some services may pause briefly.
Metro stations are gender-segregated during peak hours in some cases; families and couples can share spaces. Congestion is heaviest during morning and evening commutes.

items.label: Uber and Careem
items.text: Both apps operate widely in major cities (Riyadh, Jeddah, Dammam, etc.). Prices are transparent upfront, rides are tracked in real-time, and payment is digital—far preferable to unmarked street taxis.

items.label: White taxis
items.text: Official metered white taxis exist but are becoming rare; if using one, insist the meter is on or negotiate the fare beforehand. Many drivers prefer Uber/Careem as it's clearer for both parties.
Unmarked black taxis often overcharge tourists significantly; avoid hailing them on the street. Always use an app or call a licensed company if you don't know the driver.
Women traveling alone should use registered apps with driver tracking; avoid sitting in the front seat with strange drivers. Share your trip details with a trusted contact.
Surge pricing is common during peak hours and events; check the quoted fare before confirming. Some drivers may cancel rides if the price seems low; this can be frustrating but is normal.
You must have a valid International Driving Permit (IDP) alongside your home license; local driving licenses are required for residents. Rental companies will ask for both documents.
Saudi driving can be chaotic and aggressive; expect high speeds, sudden lane changes, and minimal signaling. If you're not an experienced defensive driver, using taxis or ride apps is safer.
Speed limits are enforced by camera and police; fines are substantial (500–3,000 SAR) and sometimes deducted from rental car deposits. Seatbelts are mandatory for all occupants.
Driving under the influence, using a phone while driving, and parking violations all carry heavy fines. Traffic accidents can result in police detention; have comprehensive travel insurance.

items.label: Fuel and rentals
items.text: Petrol is extremely cheap (roughly 0.9 SAR/liter). Car rentals are available at airports and city centers; compare Hertz, Europcar, and local firms. Comprehensive insurance is highly recommended.

items.label: Major hubs
items.text: King Abdulaziz International Airport (Jeddah), King Khalid International Airport (Riyadh), and King Fahd International Airport (Dammam) are the main gateways. All are modern, well-organized, and have good ground transport links.

items.label: Getting to/from airports
items.text: Use Uber or Careem from airports (usually cheaper than official taxis). Riyadh airport has a rapid transit rail link to the city center; check if it's operational. Hotels often offer shuttle services.
Visa requirements depend on your nationality; check with the Saudi embassy beforehand. E-visas are available for many nationalities and can be obtained online before arrival.
Airport queues can be long during peak hours; arrive early. Duty-free allowances are standard; alcohol and pork products are strictly prohibited on entry.

items.label: Prayer rooms and facilities
items.text: All major airports have prayer rooms, restaurants, prayer spaces, and family rest areas. Plan accordingly during prayer times when some facilities may be busy.

items.label: Limited ferry services
items.text: Saudi Arabia has few passenger ferry routes compared to other Gulf countries. The main option is the Dammam-Bahrain causeway crossing, primarily served by buses and cars rather than dedicated ferries.

items.label: Regional crossings
items.text: Some cruise services operate from Jeddah and Dammam to neighboring countries, but these are typically tourist cruises rather than practical transport. Check local operators for current schedules.
Ferries to other GCC countries are not a standard transport option for tourists; flights and the causeway are far more common. Don't rely on ferries as a primary means of regional travel.

items.label: Hayat smart card (Riyadh)
items.text: This reloadable card works on the Riyadh Metro and some bus services. Load it at kiosks or via the Hayat app; prices are discounted compared to single journey tickets.

items.label: Booking in advance
items.text: Purchase train and intercity bus tickets online via the Saudi Railways app, SAPTCO website, or apps like Almosafer and Klook. Early booking often yields discounts and guarantees seating.
Printed or digital tickets are both accepted; keep your booking reference and ID handy. Cancellation and refund policies vary; read terms carefully when booking.
Ticket prices surge during peak seasons (Eid holidays, summer vacation). Plan trips during off-peak times if you're budget-conscious.

items.label: Major providers
items.text: STC, Mobily, and Zain are the three main mobile operators with extensive 4G/5G coverage nationwide. All offer prepaid and postpaid plans; visitor packages are available at airports and retail shops.

items.label: Getting a SIM card
items.text: Purchase a local SIM at airport kiosks, shopping malls, or convenience stores with your passport. Activation is usually instant; data plans are inexpensive (often 30–100 SAR for generous data bundles).

items.label: Internet speed
items.text: Saudi Arabia has excellent mobile internet coverage and speeds in cities. Wifi is widely available in hotels, restaurants, malls, and public spaces; download maps offline if traveling to remote areas.
Some government websites and banking apps may require a local phone number; having a local SIM simplifies verification and two-factor authentication.
International roaming can be expensive; a local SIM is far cheaper for extended stays. Certain apps and content may be restricted or filtered depending on local regulations.
